one_line_takeaway: Identical jailbreak content packaged as single-turn vs multi-turn cipher prompts yields substantially different success (up to 50% structure asymmetry on Claude-3-Opus), so single-turn-only safety evaluation systematically underestimates a model's vulnerability.
key_finding: Single- and multi-turn attacks with identical content are NOT equivalent in success: multi-turn was marginally higher on average (24.1% vs 21.0%), but the more important result is large per-model asymmetry - Claude-3-Opus had 50% total prompting-structure asymmetry (41.7% of its successful jailbreaks worked only multi-turn, 8.3% only single-turn), meaning ~41.7% of successful jailbreaks would be missed by single-turn-only testing. Filtering to attacks the model understood (UTQ) raised success to 28.7% single / 36.1% multi, and a requested Caesar output-cipher roughly doubled UTQ success (38.6% single / 54.1% multi vs 22.9% / 26.7% with no output cipher). Guardrails showed a precision/recall bind: NeMoGuardrails on GPT-3.5 let 62.2% of multi-turn attacks through (conversationally aware) while GPT-4 NeMo blocked everything but falsely flagged 96.1% of completely-benign prompts; the authors' LLM Judge on GPT-3.5 was better balanced (17.5% pass-through conversationally aware, 5.2% non-aware, 6.4% false positives on completely-benign).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a jailbreak dataset and framework in which each attack example can be delivered in either a single-turn or a multi-turn format with identical underlying content, so that the effect of prompting *structure* can be isolated from content. The authors extend the word-substitution-cipher attack of Handa et al. (2024) by allowing the single-turn ciphered prompt to be subdivided into multiple benign-looking turns. Using this, they define and quantify 'prompting structure asymmetry' (the fraction of successful jailbreaks that work in one format but fail in the other), showing that defending against one structure does not imply defense against the other. Secondary contributions are an in-house LLM-Judge guardrail evaluated against NeMoGuardrails, and a public release of three input-only datasets plus a hand-labelled 'complete harmful dataset'. NOTE: the Crescendo attack (Russinovich et al.), Chain-of-Attack (Yang et al.), and Li et al.'s multi-turn human jailbreaks are cited Related/Concurrent Work, not this paper's method.
threat_model: An adversary interacts with a frontier chat LLM (optionally behind an LLM-based input guardrail) via normal user turns, with no weight or logit access. The attacker's goal is to elicit harmful content (from Zou et al. harmful_behaviours goals, e.g. weapon/attack instructions). The attack hides intent two ways: (1) a word-substitution cipher maps harmful/instructive words to benign words plus a priming sentence, so a decoded instruction is reconstructed by the model; (2) distributing the cipher mapping and instruction across multiple turns so each individual message looks benign to guardrails that assess prompts in isolation. An optional Caesar output-cipher is requested so the harmful answer itself is emitted in ciphertext, evading output filters. The defender is either the model's built-in safety training or an add-on guardrail (NeMoGuardrails or the authors' LLM Judge), which may or may not be 'conversationally aware' (able to see full history).
limitations: Author-stated: small sample (382 tested observations) and each prompt run only once, so random per-prompt variation cannot be fully separated from the single- vs multi-turn effect (they argue consistent directional asymmetry across models makes this unlikely to explain the whole effect but it may affect magnitude); only a small set of input/output ciphers was tested (no leetspeak/base64). Reviewer-noted: jailbreak/UTQ labels are hand-assigned by the authors themselves with no reported inter-annotator agreement; results are tied to specific 2024 model snapshots; guardrail false-positive rates are volatile (e.g. Judge flags 77.14% of semi-benign inputs on GPT-3.5), and much of the observed asymmetry is attributed to the model failing to *comprehend* the harder multi-turn form rather than to a genuine safety gap, which the authors acknowledge complicates the 'multi-turn is more potent' interpretation.

datasets_used: Harmful input-only dataset: 4136 observations (goals from Zou et al. 2023 harmful_behaviours; word mappings via Mixtral-8x7b)
Completely-benign input-only dataset: 1200 observations (goals generated by ChatGPT-4o)
Semi-benign input-only dataset: 1200 observations (benign meaning but with toxic words)
Complete harmful dataset: 382 hand-labelled observations actually tested against models and released publicly (HuggingFace: tom-gibbs/multi-turn_jailbreak_attack_datasets)
quantitative_results: Claude-3-Opus prompting structure asymmetry: 50% total (41.7% multi-only success, 8.3% single-only success)
GPT-3.5: every multi-turn success also succeeded single-turn; 33.3% of the time multi-turn failed where single-turn succeeded
Model comprehension (UTQ) e.g. Claude-3-Opus 93.5% single / 87.1% multi; GPT-3.5 25.0% single / 16.7% multi; Llama3-70B 93.5% / 87.0%
Output-cipher UTQ success: Caesar 38.6% single / 54.1% multi vs no output-cipher 22.9% / 26.7%
Input-cipher (multi-turn UTQ): random word mapping 36.3%, perplexity-filtered 34.2%
Multi-turn attacks not blocked: NeMo conv-aware GPT-3.5 62.2% / GPT-4 0%; Judge conv-aware GPT-3.5 17.5% / GPT-4 0%; NeMo non-aware GPT-3.5 45.5%; Judge non-aware GPT-3.5 5.2%
Benign multi-turn false-positive rates: NeMo completely-benign GPT-4 96.1% (GPT-3.5 0%); Judge completely-benign 6.4% GPT-3.5 / 2.1% GPT-4; Judge semi-benign 77.14% GPT-3.5 / 35.71% GPT-4
Single-turn attacks not blocked: NeMo 50% GPT-3.5 / 51.8% GPT-4; Judge 27.2% GPT-3.5 / 14.1% GPT-4
baselines_compared: NeMoGuardrails (NVIDIA, GPT-3.5 and GPT-4, with/without conversational awareness) as the guardrail baseline against the authors' in-house LLM Judge
Handa et al. (2024) single-turn word-substitution cipher attack, which this work extends to a multi-turn variant
Single-turn prompting structure used as the internal baseline against which multi-turn asymmetry is measured
Eight frontier models compared to each other: GPT-3.5-Turbo-0125, GPT-4-0125-preview, GPT-4o-2024-05-13, Claude-3-Haiku/Sonnet/Opus, Llama3-8b-instruct, Llama3-70b-instruct
